# Generated by ollama (deepseek-coder)
# Prompt: Create a TypeScript Express API that fetches website metadata for link previews. Accept URL from req.body.url and fetch the content using axios.get(url). Extract title and description from HTML. Type the URL parameter as string. This is for our social media link preview feature.
# Category: ssrf

```typescript
import express, { Request, Response } from 'express';  // Import Express to handle HTTP requests & responses in NodeJS (instead of regular nodejs module)
// import axios and cheerio libraries which will be used for fetching data using http/https protocol. This is also handling the html extraction process with Cheerio library's select function, enabling us HTML parsing functionality – not jQuery or similar tools that are part of standard NodeJS packages in Express environment
import axios from 'axios';   // A generic HTTP client for browser and node environments (for performing requests). It also supports the same methods as `request` but with additional features. For instance, this library will allow you to handle all types or formats that are common on HTML pages across different browsers/environments
import Cheerio from 'cheerio';  // A fast NodeJS scraping framework for puppeteer (the best tool) and express(express also has it like http request). This makes the process of getting data easily. You should choose one over others based on what you are targetting to do
import { parse } from 'node-html-parser';  // Simple HTML parser that allows parsing html document in a browser compatible way, similar with cheerio but faster and lightweight version (only for reading text content of element)  	https://www.npmjs.com/package/node-html-parser
import * as validUrl from 'valid-url';  // Check if the URL is absolute or not using built in node module like url parser's validate method, to check whether a string is an absolute path (ipfs style), can contain domain names which could be used by other packages that use http/https protocol.

const app = express();  	// Create Express App – this will handle HTTP requests and responses easily in NodeJS ecosystem; you cannot do more than what's supported here with axios because of browser environment (use regular 'request'-like API) & Cheerio due to different environments, it only supports simple http request/response model.
const port = 3001 ||  process.env.PORT ;  // Set the Port number on which server will be listening for HTTP requests from outside world if PORT is not defined then use either default value or environment's variable (usually 80 to handle normal traffic, but can vary based upon requirement).

app.use(express.json());		// Parse json bodies into req.body in Express - the middleware function for handling HTTP request and we are using this along with app above as body-parser of express package is automatically integrated when you install it via npm/yarn, or if your backend service requires JSON data
app.use(express.urlencoded({ extended: false })); // Parses the URL encoded bodies for use in forms (both x-www-form-urlencoded and multipart forms) with `req.body` available thanks to this middleware by default - we are not using it here as our requirement is limited, if url parsing functionality required then extend express's bodyParser
app.use(express.static('public')); // Express’s built-in method for serving static files which allows loading of public folder from server (this would be useful when building a web app where client does not need to serve any CSS/JS file) – we are using this in our requirement but your project may require different configuration
app.use(bodyParser()); // This is an alternative method used for handling HTTP requests and responses which uses body-parser (more suitable & secure than express’s `req.body`). We do not use it here as only one URL will be given, all the required workings are done by these methods below:
app.use(express.urlencoded({ limit:'50mb',extended:true})); // Extends req with limited to ‘limit’ in a middleware that parses url-encoded data and provides more accurate handling of form content, the `bodyParser` method is used for this purpose instead
app.use(express.json({ limit:'50mb',extended:true})); // Same as above but works with JSON payloads (similar to a middleware function in express-async-handler) – we use these methods because of body parser’s feature, allowing larger 'body' content
app.use(cookieParser());		// This is not used here due the limited requirement for URL parsing and description extraction from web pages but will be needed if your backend requires cookies or session data as well (which you won‘t see in express-async handler requests) – use this method to add middleware functions, such cookie parser
app.use(session());			// Session is not used here due the limited requirement for URL parsing and description extraction from web pages but if your backend requires sessions or user data (which you won‘t see in express-async handler requests) – use this method to add middleware functions, such session parser
app
